skill.name: ntm
skill.description: Named Tmux Manager coordinates Claude Code, Codex, and Gemini agents inside tmux sessions with dashboards, prompts, robot mode, and workflow automation.
skill.summary: Coordinate multiple AI coding agents in persistent tmux workspaces.
skill.author: Dicklesworthstone

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command-execution patterns; most are Markdown command examples for an orchestration CLI, not executable code inside the skill. However, the documentation includes remote pipe-to-shell installation, shell startup modification, arbitrary command hooks, Slack webhooks, and agent launch flags that bypass safety controls. No prompt injection attempt or confirmed malware intent was found, but the skill is high risk for marketplace publication.

high_findings.title: Remote Installer Piped to Shell
high_findings.description: The skill recommends downloading an installer from a GitHub raw URL and piping it directly to bash. This is a true positive because a marketplace user may execute unreviewed remote code with the privileges of their shell.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact pipe-to-shell pattern appears twice in documented install commands. It is a common installer pattern, but it still creates clear remote code execution risk.

high_findings.title: Agent Safety Controls Disabled in Example Configuration
high_findings.description: The example configuration launches Claude, Codex, and Gemini with flags that skip permissions, bypass approvals and sandboxing, or run in yolo mode. This is a true positive because it encourages agents to run with reduced safeguards across a user workspace.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The configuration explicitly names permissive flags for three agent CLIs. The semantic context confirms this is an intended operating mode, not a scanner artifact.

medium_findings.title: Shell Startup File Modification
medium_findings.description: The quick start appends shell initialization output to a hidden startup file and sources it immediately. This is a true positive for filesystem and command execution risk because persistent shell behavior changes can affect future terminal sessions.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line directly modifies a user shell startup file. It appears to be normal shell integration, but the persistence risk is real.

medium_findings.title: Arbitrary Command Hook Execution
medium_findings.description: The command hooks section documents configurable shell commands for lifecycle events, including a pre-send hook that writes message data to a hidden log file. This is a true positive because hooks can execute arbitrary shell commands and may process untrusted prompt content.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The hook configuration is explicit and includes command substitution and an environment variable. Legitimate hook usage is possible, but the command-execution surface is clear.

medium_findings.title: Webhook Notification Configuration
medium_findings.description: The notifications example enables a webhook and uses a Slack hooks URL placeholder. This is a true positive for network risk because agent events or operational metadata could be sent to an external service if configured.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL is a placeholder, not a live secret. The risk is still credible because the feature is designed to send event notifications to a network endpoint.

low_findings.title: Dangerous Command Strings Used as Blocklist Examples
low_findings.description: The static scanner flagged destructive strings such as recursive root deletion and database destruction. These are false positives in context because the skill lists them as protected commands that the safety system should block.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding text states that these patterns are blocked dangerous commands. This confirms they are defensive examples, not instructions to run them.

low_findings.title: Numerous CLI Examples Misclassified as Ruby Backticks
low_findings.description: Most external command findings are Markdown examples documenting how to use the NTM CLI. They are not executable code in the skill file, but they still indicate that the skill is centered on process orchestration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The affected lines are fenced Markdown examples and command tables. This makes the scanner label mostly false positive, while preserving a low contextual risk note.

dangerous_patterns.title: Pipe Remote Content to Shell
dangerous_patterns.description: The command pattern downloads a remote installer and immediately executes it with bash, creating supply-chain and remote execution risk.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is directly present and has well-known execution risk. The source is a GitHub raw URL, which is not inherently malicious but requires review.

dangerous_patterns.title: Persistent Shell Eval Integration
dangerous_patterns.description: The command appends an eval-based initialization snippet to a shell startup file, making NTM-generated shell code run in later sessions.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command writes to a persistent hidden startup file. It is normal for CLI completion or alias setup, but it expands trust in generated shell code.

dangerous_patterns.title: Permission Bypass Agent Launch Flags
dangerous_patterns.description: The example agent configuration disables or bypasses permission and sandbox controls for multiple AI CLIs.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The permissive flags are explicit and central to the example configuration. This is the strongest semantic risk in the skill content.

content.value_statement: Managing several coding agents across terminals creates fragmented sessions and weak visibility. This skill documents NTM workflows for coordinating Claude Code, Codex, and Gemini agents in persistent tmux sessions.
actual_capabilities: Documents commands for spawning Claude Code, Codex, and Gemini panes in named tmux sessions.
Shows how to send prompts to one agent type or all active agents.
Describes dashboard, palette, activity, and status views for multi-agent sessions.
Explains output capture, code extraction, grep, diff, and session analytics workflows.
Covers robot mode commands for machine-readable status, context, work, and health checks.
Documents integrations for agent mail, work assignment, hooks, alerts, and context rotation.
limitations: The skill is documentation only and does not include the NTM Go CLI implementation.
It assumes tmux and the selected AI command-line tools are already installed.
Several examples use permissive agent modes that may bypass local safety controls.
Robot mode and integrations depend on external tools that may not exist in every project.

use_cases.title: Coordinate parallel implementation work
use_cases.description: Run separate agents for architecture, implementation, review, and tests inside one tmux session.

use_cases.title: Monitor long-running agent sessions
use_cases.description: Use dashboards, activity views, context indicators, and health checks during remote development.

use_cases.title: Automate agent status collection
use_cases.description: Use robot mode commands to collect session state, recent output, file changes, and work plans.

prompt_templates.title: Start a basic agent session
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for one project workspace.
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me a safe NTM workflow for creating a small tmux session with one Claude Code pane and one Codex pane.

prompt_templates.title: Assign work across agents
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate coordination for implementation and review.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an NTM command plan that assigns design, implementation, review, and testing work across Claude Code, Codex, and Gemini.

prompt_templates.title: Inspect session health
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced monitoring during a long-running session.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a monitoring checklist using NTM dashboard, activity, context, output capture, and health commands for an active multi-agent session.

prompt_templates.title: Build robot mode automation
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan a robot mode automation that gathers NTM session state, checks agent context usage, reviews file changes, and reports blocked work.

output_examples.input: Ask for a safe startup plan for two agents in one project.
output_examples.output: A concise sequence that checks dependencies, creates a named session, starts one Claude Code pane and one Codex pane, then opens the dashboard.

output_examples.input: Ask how to compare work from two agents.
output_examples.output: A workflow that sends the same task to selected agents, captures their pane output, compares the results, and routes review feedback.

output_examples.input: Ask how to monitor context pressure during a long task.
output_examples.output: A checklist covering context indicators, activity states, compaction recovery, rotation thresholds, and handoff summaries.

best_practices: Review generated commands before running them in a real repository.
Use least-privilege agent settings instead of permissive bypass flags when possible.
Start with small sessions before coordinating many agents in parallel.
anti_patterns: Do not pipe remote installers to a shell without reviewing the installer source.
Do not enable shell hooks that process untrusted messages without validation.
Do not run multiple agents with full filesystem access in sensitive repositories.

faq.question: Does this skill install NTM?
faq.answer: No. It documents NTM commands and workflows, but the actual CLI must be installed separately.

faq.question: Which agents does it describe?
faq.answer: It describes workflows for Claude Code, Codex, and Gemini command-line agents.

faq.question: Can it run without tmux?
faq.answer: No. The documented workflows depend on tmux sessions and panes.

faq.question: Is robot mode for humans or automation?
faq.answer: Robot mode is intended for automation that needs machine-readable session, pane, context, and work state.

faq.question: Why is the security risk high?
faq.answer: The skill documents remote shell installation, shell startup modification, arbitrary command hooks, and permissive agent launch flags.

faq.question: Can teams use safer settings?
faq.answer: Yes. Teams should avoid bypass flags, review hooks, and restrict access before using NTM in sensitive projects.
